title: Demand and the law of demand
summary: Demand is the first half of the market mechanism. This lesson builds the concept of effective demand, the law of demand and the reasoning behind it (the income and substitution effects and diminishing marginal utility), the demand curve, and the derivation of market demand from individual demand. It then draws the single most examined distinction in microeconomics — a movement ALONG the curve versus a SHIFT of the whole curve — and works through every non-price determinant that shifts it.
learningObjectives: Define demand and state the law of demand, explaining it through the income effect, the substitution effect and diminishing marginal utility.
Draw and interpret a demand curve, and derive a market demand curve by horizontally summing individual demand curves.
Distinguish precisely between a change in quantity demanded (a movement along the curve) and a change in demand (a shift of the curve).
Explain each non-price determinant of demand and predict the direction of the shift it causes.
levelNote: Content is common to SL and HL at this level; HL extensions appear later in the unit.

simpleExplanation.title: Why We Buy More When Prices Drop
simpleExplanation.summary: Demand is how much of something people are willing AND able to buy at each possible price. The law of demand says that, other things equal, a lower price leads people to buy more and a higher price leads them to buy less — which is why the demand curve slopes downwards.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about your favourite takeaway pizza. If a promotion drops the price from £12 to £8, you and your friends order more this Friday. If it jumped to £20, you'd switch to something else. That is a movement along your demand curve — driven by the pizza's own price. But if your part-time wage went up, you might order more pizza at EVERY price — that is a shift of the whole curve.
steps: Demand is not just wanting — it is being willing AND able to buy at a given price (effective demand).
The law of demand: as price falls, quantity demanded rises, giving a downward-sloping curve.
A change in the good's OWN price causes a movement ALONG the curve (a change in quantity demanded).
A change in any OTHER factor (income, related-good prices, tastes...) SHIFTS the whole curve left or right (a change in demand).

flashcards.back: The quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to purchase at each price over a given period, ceteris paribus.
A want backed by the ability and willingness to pay. Economics studies effective demand, not mere desire — wishing for a Ferrari you cannot afford is not demand.
Ceteris paribus, there is an inverse relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded: as price rises, quantity demanded falls, and vice versa.
Quantity demanded is a SINGLE point (one price, one amount). Demand is the WHOLE relationship — the entire curve. A price change moves you along the curve; a non-price change shifts the whole curve.
A graph of the relationship between price (vertical axis) and quantity demanded (horizontal axis). It slopes downwards from left to right.
The total quantity demanded by all consumers in a market at each price. It is found by HORIZONTALLY summing the individual demand curves (adding quantities at each price).
A change in QUANTITY DEMANDED caused ONLY by a change in the good's own price — e.g. moving from point A to point B on the same curve.
A change in DEMAND at every price, caused by a non-price determinant. The whole curve moves right (increase) or left (decrease), from D1 to D2.
As you consume more of a good, the extra satisfaction (utility) from each additional unit falls. So consumers will only buy more units at a lower price — one reason demand slopes downwards.
A fall in price raises real income (purchasing power), so consumers can afford to buy more of the good — reinforcing the law of demand.
A fall in price makes the good cheaper RELATIVE to substitutes, so consumers switch spending towards it — reinforcing the law of demand.
For a NORMAL good, higher income raises demand (shifts right). For an INFERIOR good, higher income LOWERS demand (shifts left) as consumers trade up to better alternatives.
SUBSTITUTES are used in place of each other (a rise in the price of one raises demand for the other). COMPLEMENTS are used together (a rise in the price of one lowers demand for the other).
Latin for 'all other things being equal'. The assumption that lets us isolate the effect of price on quantity demanded while holding non-price factors constant.

quickCheck.prompt: A café lowers the price of a latte and sells more lattes as a result. In demand analysis this is:
options: An increase in demand (rightward shift)
An increase in quantity demanded (movement along the curve)
A decrease in demand (leftward shift)
A shift caused by a non-price determinant
quickCheck.answer: An increase in quantity demanded (movement along the curve)

quickCheck.prompt: Consumers' incomes rise and, at every price, they now buy more restaurant meals. Restaurant meals are a normal good, so this is shown by:
options: A movement up along the demand curve
A movement down along the demand curve
A rightward shift of the whole demand curve
A leftward shift of the whole demand curve
quickCheck.answer: A rightward shift of the whole demand curve

quickCheck.prompt: Which change causes a MOVEMENT ALONG the demand curve for tea rather than a shift of it?
options: A change in the price of tea itself
A change in the price of coffee (a substitute)
A change in consumers' incomes
A successful advertising campaign for tea
quickCheck.answer: A change in the price of tea itself

quickCheck.prompt: The price of printer ink (a complement to printers) rises sharply. The most likely effect on the demand for printers is:
options: Demand for printers shifts left (decreases)
Demand for printers shifts right (increases)
Quantity of printers demanded rises along the curve
No effect, because ink is not a printer

quickCheck.prompt: 'When the price of a good falls, demand increases.' Why is this statement imprecise for exam purposes?
options: It should say quantity demanded increases — a price change is a movement along the curve, not a shift in demand
It should say demand decreases, because lower prices reduce revenue
It is correct as written
Price changes never affect quantity demanded
quickCheck.answer: It should say quantity demanded increases — a price change is a movement along the curve, not a shift in demand

faq.q: What is the difference between 'demand' and 'quantity demanded'?
faq.a: This is the distinction examiners test most often. 'Quantity demanded' is a specific amount consumers will buy at ONE specific price — a single point on the curve. 'Demand' is the ENTIRE relationship between every possible price and its corresponding quantity — the whole curve. A change in the good's own price changes the quantity demanded (a movement along the curve); a change in a non-price factor changes demand (a shift of the whole curve).

faq.q: Why is the demand curve downward sloping?
faq.a: Three reinforcing reasons. (1) The income effect: a lower price raises real purchasing power, so consumers can afford more. (2) The substitution effect: a lower price makes the good cheaper relative to substitutes, so consumers switch towards it. (3) Diminishing marginal utility: each extra unit consumed yields less additional satisfaction, so consumers will only buy more units at a lower price.

faq.q: Can a demand curve ever slope upwards?
faq.a: In theory, rarely. A 'Giffen good' is a strongly inferior good for which the income effect of a price rise outweighs the substitution effect, so people buy more as price rises. 'Veblen' (status) goods can behave similarly. For IB SL you should assume demand slopes downwards unless a question explicitly says otherwise.

faq.q: If my income rises, does my demand for every good rise?
faq.a: No. For NORMAL goods (e.g. restaurant meals, new technology) demand shifts right when income rises. For INFERIOR goods (e.g. instant noodles, long-distance bus travel) demand shifts left, because consumers trade up to alternatives they can now afford. Whether a good is normal or inferior depends on the consumer and their income level.

sections.content: Welcome to the study of demand, one of the two blades of the market mechanism. Why do consumers buy more of a good when it gets cheaper? What makes them want more of it even when the price has not changed? This lesson builds the concept of demand precisely, explains the reasoning behind the law of demand, and then draws the distinction that more IB marks hinge on than almost any other: the difference between moving ALONG a demand curve and SHIFTING the whole curve.
What economists mean by demand

In economics 'demand' has a precise meaning — it is not simply a want or a desire. Demand is the quantity of a good that consumers are both WILLING and ABLE to buy over a range of prices in a given period. This is called effective demand: if you would love a Ferrari but cannot pay for one, you have desire but not demand. Demand analysis is almost always conducted under the ceteris paribus assumption — 'all other things being equal' — so that we can isolate the effect of a single variable, usually price, while holding every other influence constant.
The law of demand

The law of demand states that, ceteris paribus, as the price of a good falls the quantity demanded rises, and as the price rises the quantity demanded falls. This inverse relationship rests on three reinforcing ideas. The INCOME EFFECT: a lower price raises the real purchasing power of a consumer's income, so they can afford to buy more. The SUBSTITUTION EFFECT: a lower price makes the good cheaper relative to its substitutes, so consumers switch their spending towards it. And DIMINISHING MARGINAL UTILITY: each additional unit consumed yields less extra satisfaction than the last, so a rational consumer will only buy those later, less-valued units if the price is lower. Together these explain why the demand curve slopes downwards.

items: Inverse relationship: Price ↑ → Quantity demanded ↓, and Price ↓ → Quantity demanded ↑.
Holds under the ceteris paribus assumption (non-price factors constant).
Explained by the income effect, the substitution effect, and diminishing marginal utility.
The result is a downward-sloping demand curve, with price on the vertical axis and quantity on the horizontal axis.

sections.content: The intuition of diminishing marginal utility

Utility is the satisfaction a consumer gets from consuming a good. Marginal utility is the EXTRA satisfaction from one more unit. The first cold drink on a hot day gives enormous satisfaction; the second is still welcome; by the fourth or fifth the extra satisfaction is small. Because each additional unit is worth less to the consumer, they are only willing to pay a lower price for it. This is the microeconomic engine behind the downward slope: to persuade a consumer to buy extra, lower-valued units, the price must fall — which is exactly the law of demand seen from the consumer's side.

sections.question:
Zara's weekly demand for cups of coffee at a local café is shown below.

| Price (£) | Quantity demanded |
|---|---|
| 4.00 | 1 |
| 3.50 | 3 |
| 3.00 | 5 |
| 2.50 | 7 |
| 2.00 | 9 |

(a) Describe how you would draw and label Zara's demand curve. [2]
(b) The price falls from £3.00 to £2.50. Identify and name the change this causes. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a)** Plot price on the vertical (y) axis and quantity demanded on the horizontal (x) axis. Each row is a point: (1, 4.00), (3, 3.50), (5, 3.00), (7, 2.50), (9, 2.00). Join them to form a downward-sloping line.
- [1] Axes correctly labelled — 'Price (£)' on the y-axis, 'Quantity demanded' on the x-axis, and the curve labelled 'D'.
- [1] Points plotted accurately and joined into a downward-sloping curve.

**(b)** At £3.00 the quantity demanded is 5 cups; at £2.50 it is 7 cups. So quantity demanded RISES by 2 cups.
- [1] Correctly states quantity demanded increases by 2 cups.
- [1] Correctly NAMES this a movement ALONG the demand curve (a change in quantity demanded), because it was caused by the good's OWN price changing — not a shift in demand.

sections.content: From individual to market demand

Individual demand matters, but firms and governments care about MARKET demand — the total demand from all consumers for a good. To find it we add up the quantities every individual demands at each price. This is called HORIZONTAL summation because we add along the quantity (horizontal) axis, price by price. If different consumers enter the market at different prices, the market curve can have 'kinks', but it remains downward sloping overall.

sections.question:
A market for concert tickets has just two consumers, Anya and Ben.

| Price (£) | Anya's Qd | Ben's Qd |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0 | 1 |
| 80 | 1 | 2 |
| 60 | 2 | 3 |
| 40 | 3 | 4 |

(a) Derive the market demand schedule. [2]
(b) State the market quantity demanded at £60 and explain the method used. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a)** Sum the two individual quantities at each price:

| Price (£) | Anya | Ben | Market Qd |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 80 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| 60 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| 40 | 3 | 4 | 7 |

- [1] Shows the method of adding the two individual quantities at each price.
- [1] All four market totals correct (1, 3, 5, 7).

**(b)** At £60 the market quantity demanded is 5 tickets (Anya's 2 + Ben's 3). The method is HORIZONTAL summation: at each price we add the quantities demanded by every consumer along the horizontal axis to obtain the market total.
- [1] Correct figure of 5.
- [1] Correct explanation of horizontal summation.

Notice Anya only enters the market at £80 or below, so above £80 the market curve reflects Ben's demand alone.

sections.content: Movements along vs shifts of the demand curve

This is the single most examined distinction in the topic. A change in QUANTITY DEMANDED is a MOVEMENT ALONG the existing demand curve, and it is caused ONLY by a change in the good's OWN price — for example, a shop cutting the price of a chocolate bar moves consumers down along the curve for that bar.

A change in DEMAND is a SHIFT of the whole curve — right for an increase, left for a decrease. It means that at EVERY price consumers now want to buy more (or less) than before, and it is caused by a change in any factor OTHER than the good's own price. These factors are the non-price determinants of demand.

items: Change in QUANTITY DEMANDED = movement along the curve = caused by the good's OWN price.
Change in DEMAND = shift of the whole curve = caused by a NON-PRICE determinant.
A rightward shift (D1→D2) is an INCREASE in demand; a leftward shift is a DECREASE.
Label a shift with two curves (D1, D2) and an arrow; label a movement with two points (A, B) on ONE curve.

sections.content: Never write 'demand goes up' when you mean a price change. Say 'quantity demanded increases/decreases' for a movement along the curve, and 'demand shifts right/left' for a shift. On diagrams, show a movement as two points on ONE curve, and a shift as a second curve (D1 to D2) with a directional arrow. Getting this vocabulary right is often the difference between the middle and top mark bands.
The non-price determinants of demand
These are the factors that shift the whole demand curve. A helpful mnemonic is PIRATES:

items: **P**opulation: more consumers in the market shift demand right; fewer shift it left.
**I**ncome: for a NORMAL good, higher income shifts demand right; for an INFERIOR good, higher income shifts demand left.
**R**elated goods: for SUBSTITUTES, a rise in the price of good A shifts demand for good B right; for COMPLEMENTS, a rise in the price of good A shifts demand for good B left.
**A**dvertising and branding: effective campaigns raise consumer loyalty and shift demand right.
**T**astes and preferences: fashions, trends and health awareness shift demand either way.
**E**xpectations: expecting future price rises can shift current demand right; expecting price falls can shift it left.
**S**easons: demand for goods such as ice cream, winter coats or holiday decorations varies with the time of year.

sections.content: To analyse a non-price change, use this chain: (1) identify the determinant; (2) state how it has changed; (3) state the effect on demand (increase/decrease) and the direction of shift (right/left); (4) illustrate with a fully labelled diagram showing D1 shifting to D2. Steps 3 and 4 are where most marks are won.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing quantity demanded with demand** — a change in the good's OWN price changes quantity demanded (a movement), NOT demand (a shift). Writing 'lower price increases demand' loses marks.

**Confusing a movement ALONG the curve with a SHIFT of the curve** — draw a movement as two points on one curve and a shift as a new curve D1→D2; mixing them up is the most common error in this topic.
**Treating demand as mere desire** — economics studies EFFECTIVE demand (willing AND able to pay), not wants.

**Wrong direction for related goods** — a rise in the price of a SUBSTITUTE increases demand for the other good (shift right); a rise in the price of a COMPLEMENT decreases it (shift left). Do not swap these.
**Assuming higher income always raises demand** — it does for normal goods but LOWERS demand for inferior goods.
**Unlabelled diagrams** — omitting axis labels, the curve label, or the shift arrow caps your marks even when the analysis is sound.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using a demand diagram, the law of demand and TWO non-price determinants that could shift the demand curve. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
The law of demand states that, ceteris paribus, there is an inverse relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded: as price falls, quantity demanded rises, and as price rises, quantity demanded falls. This is explained by the income effect (a lower price raises real purchasing power, so consumers can afford more), the substitution effect (the good becomes cheaper relative to substitutes, so consumers switch towards it), and diminishing marginal utility (each extra unit yields less satisfaction, so consumers will only buy more at a lower price).

*[Diagram: a demand curve D1 with 'Price' on the vertical axis and 'Quantity' on the horizontal axis, sloping downward from left to right. A fall in price from P1 to P2 is shown as a movement DOWN along D1 from point A to point B, with quantity rising from Q1 to Q2 — this illustrates the law of demand. A second curve D2 is drawn to the RIGHT of D1, with an arrow from D1 to D2, to illustrate an INCREASE in demand caused by a non-price determinant.]*

The law of demand alone produces only movements along a single curve (A to B). By contrast, a NON-PRICE determinant shifts the whole curve. First, INCOME: if the good is normal, a rise in consumer incomes means that at every price more is demanded, so the curve shifts right from D1 to D2. Second, the PRICE OF A RELATED GOOD: if the price of a substitute rises, consumers switch to this good, so again demand shifts right from D1 to D2; conversely a rise in the price of a complement would shift demand left. In each case the shift occurs at every price, which is why it is a change in DEMAND (a shift) and not merely a change in quantity demanded (a movement).

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9–10:** demands addressed, theory FULLY explained, terms appropriate THROUGHOUT, diagram(s) included and FULLY explained.
- **7–8:** theory explained, terms mostly appropriate, diagram included and explained.
- **5–6:** demands partially addressed, theory PARTLY explained, some terms, diagram included.
- **3–4:** some understanding, theory DESCRIBED, some terms.
- **1–2:** little understanding, theory/terms not relevant.

Why the model answer reaches 9–10:
- ✅ Both demands addressed — the law of demand AND two named non-price determinants (income; price of a related good).
- ✅ Theory FULLY explained — the law is justified through the income effect, substitution effect and diminishing marginal utility, not merely stated.
- ✅ Terms used appropriately THROUGHOUT — ceteris paribus, quantity demanded, shift versus movement, normal good, substitute/complement.
- ✅ Diagram included AND fully explained — axes and curves (D1, D2) labelled, the A→B movement used to show the law, and the D1→D2 shift used to show each determinant, with the movement-versus-shift distinction made explicit.
An answer that only DEFINED the law and mentioned the determinants with an unlabelled or unexplained diagram would sit in the 3–4 ('theory described') or 5–6 ('partly explained') band — the top band is reached by EXPLAINING the diagram, not just drawing it.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this SAME markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The single habit that moves you up the bands here: separate the MOVEMENT (law of demand, A→B on one curve) from the SHIFT (non-price determinants, D1→D2), and explain your diagram rather than just drawing it.
Where this leads

Demand is only half of the story. In the next lessons you will meet supply — the mirror-image behaviour of producers — and then bring the two together to find market equilibrium, where the price and quantity are actually determined. Every idea here (effective demand, the law of demand, movements versus shifts) returns the moment we start moving markets towards, and away from, equilibrium.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: use a demand diagram to explain the law of demand and two non-price determinants

items.label: Khan Academy: Demand, Supply and Market Equilibrium
